Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place this semester. I tried to set up a schedule where I'd study about three hours a day, but between my extracurriculars and just feeling overwhelmed by having eleven courses online, things never stayed consistent. I'd often find myself cramming late at night when anxiety kicked in, listening to lecture recordings while trying to catch up on everything I'd missed. I wanted to be organized, but the sheer volume of work made it feel impossible to stick to any plan.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and how panicked I was feeling. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because I was paralyzed by how much there was to do, then suddenly a deadline would hit and I'd be up all night trying to catch up. I never really found that steady rhythm I was hoping for at the start of the semester. The anxiety made it hard to approach things calmly and systematically.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or realizing I'd missed something important that got me to log in. Sometimes my classmates would mention something in the discussion forums and I'd rush to check what I'd missed. I also logged in a lot just to see what was happening, even if I didn't always engage deeply with the content. It was like checking to make sure I wasn't falling even further behind than I already was.
Q4: There was a brief period early in the semester where I tried to make logging in every morning part of my routine, like checking messages or seeing what new content was posted. But that fell apart pretty quickly when I started feeling overwhelmed by the sheer amount of notifications and tasks piling up. After that, it became more reactive than routine. The CBT sessions I've been doing have helped me recognize these patterns, but changing them is still a work in progress.
Q5: The audio lectures and recorded discussions were genuinely helpful for me since I learn best by listening. I could play them while doing other things or replay parts I didn't understand the first time. The discussion forums were also useful because hearing other students' perspectives helped concepts click in ways they didn't when I just read the materials. Those twelve posts I made were actually meaningful learning moments for me.
Q6: I often skipped the longer reading materials and some of the video content because I just couldn't concentrate long enough to get through them. When you're juggling eleven courses and your anxiety is through the roof, sitting through a forty-minute video feels impossible. I'd tell myself I'd come back to them later, but later rarely came. The written resources especially felt like they required a mental bandwidth I just didn't have.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could immediately see how it connected to what I needed to know for assignments or exams. If I could listen to something and feel like I actually understood a concept better, that felt valuable. But when activities seemed disconnected from the assessments or too abstract, my brain just couldn't justify spending limited energy on them. With so much competing for my attention, everything had to prove its worth quickly.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, honestly. When you're already drowning trying to complete the required stuff, optional content just adds to the guilt pile of things you should be doing but can't. Required activities at least had the urgency of consequences attached to them, which sometimes was the only thing that could cut through my paralysis. Optional ones just made me feel worse about not doing them.
Q9: Absolutely, yes. I downloaded a lot of audio files and listened to them on my phone when I didn't have internet access, which was often since my connection at home isn't reliable. I also borrowed textbooks from friends with more resources and studied from those. Some of my best studying happened with a classmate explaining concepts to me verbally, which obviously wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times I'd be sitting with printed notes or listening to downloaded lectures for hours, genuinely trying to learn, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd also have study sessions where friends and I would talk through problems together, working through concepts verbally since that's how I learn best. My attendance in class was low, but sometimes I was studying the same material on my own time in ways that worked better for my brain.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture recording once, download it or take notes on it, and then review those notes or replay the audio offline multiple times. My internet situation meant I had to be strategic about when I could actually be online. So one login might represent hours of subsequent offline study that nobody would ever see in the data.
Q12: Around midterms, everything just crashed for me emotionally. The workload from all eleven courses peaked at the same time, and my anxiety got so bad I could barely function. I'd log into the LMS, see all the overdue items, and just freeze. That's actually when I started the CBT sessions, because I recognized I needed help. My attendance dropped significantly during that period because facing the classroom felt impossible.
Q13: Massively. When I was exhausted or confused, I'd log in but just scroll without really processing anything. The fatigue made everything feel ten times harder than it should have been. My extracurricular commitments also pulled me away at crucial times, and then I'd feel guilty and anxious, which made it even harder to engage productively. It became this vicious cycle where stress prevented engagement, and lack of engagement caused more stress.
Q14: So many times. I'd have the LMS open, clicking through pages, but my mind was completely elsewhere, consumed by worry about everything I wasn't getting done. Sometimes I'd watch videos with the sound on but not actually absorb anything because I was too anxious. Those sessions probably looked like engagement in the data, but I walked away having learned nothing. It was more like going through the motions to feel less guilty.
Q15: I avoided the bigger assignments constantly, even though I knew they mattered most. The anxiety made them feel so overwhelming that I'd do smaller, less important tasks instead just to feel like I was accomplishing something. It's like my brain couldn't handle the weight of the important stuff, so it would find any excuse to do something else. I knew it was self-sabotaging, but knowing that didn't make it easier to stop.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that could override my paralysis sometimes. I'd ignore something for weeks, then the night before it was due, panic would finally push me into action. But that meant my work was rushed and not my best, which showed in my assignment scores. The LMS probably shows these spikes of activity right before due dates, which is just a map of my anxiety patterns really.
Q17: When I got decent feedback on assignments, it motivated me briefly to try harder on the next one. But when feedback was critical or when I saw my exam scores, it often made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid the course for a while. It's complicated because I wanted to improve, but seeing evidence of how far behind I was sometimes made engagement feel pointless. The feedback loop became part of my stress cycle.
Q18: Having eleven courses with different structures and expectations made it nearly impossible to keep track of everything. Some courses had clear weekly modules which helped, but others were more chaotic. When the structure was predictable, I could at least plan my panic sessions around it. The courses that dumped everything at once or had unclear expectations were the ones where I fell behind fastest.
Q19: Courses with audio content and discussion-based activities were easier for me to engage with because of how I learn. But courses that relied heavily on reading dense texts or watching long videos without interaction were brutal. Also, when everything was due at the same time across multiple courses, it made engagement with any single course feel impossible. Staggered deadlines would have helped me enormously.
Q20: That's literally me, and I can explain it. You log in constantly because you're anxious and checking what you're missing, you click through materials trying to understand what's expected, you participate in discussions because that feels manageable. But when it comes to actually completing and submitting the big assignments, the anxiety and overwhelm prevent you from finishing. High activity can actually be a sign of struggling, not succeeding.
Q21: I can see how that happens. Some people are just more efficient, or they study effectively offline and only use the LMS for submissions. If you have good resources and learn well independently, you might not need to be constantly logged in. For me, my high activity was often unproductive anxiety-clicking, so I can imagine someone with lower activity but better focus actually learning more effectively.
Q22: The log can show when you're active and what you click on, but it can't show what's happening in your head while you're clicking. It can't measure understanding, or the hours spent studying downloaded materials, or learning from conversations with classmates. It definitely can't capture the anxiety that makes you log in constantly without actually learning, or the offline work that never gets recorded. It's like seeing footprints without knowing why the person was walking.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks to avoid consequences. It means your brain is present and processing, not just going through motions while anxiety screams in the background. For me, real engagement happened in those moments when I was listening to a lecture and something finally clicked, or when a discussion with peers made a concept come alive. It's about genuine learning, not just activity.
Q24: The LMS would never show how hard I was actually trying despite my results, or how much the anxiety was affecting everything. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline without internet, or the learning that happened through conversations with friends. It also wouldn't show that my low attendance wasn't about not caring—it was about being too overwhelmed to face the classroom. The data tells a story of failure, but it misses the story of someone fighting really hard against their own brain.
